hero.subheadline: A software engineer from Rwanda who prayed his way into coding, found his voice in Year 2, and now builds things that matter.

milestones.title: The Prayer
milestones.body: I saw one student from my school get into Rwanda Coding Academy. I made it my mission. I prayed for it every single day. Every time I had internet, I went back to the RCA website just to learn more about it. That was the level of my hunger.

milestones.title: The Admission
milestones.body: My father is a carpenter. My mother is a farmer. We are five children and I am the second born. I had never owned a PC. My English was nearly zero. I was terrified I wasn't good enough. Then the admission came. It felt like a miracle. Because it was.
milestones.pill: Top 80 admitted · O-Level · English, Math, Physics

milestones.title: Year 1 · The Quiet One
milestones.body: Everyone said: this man does not talk. My voice was barely audible in a room. I felt like the odd man out. I wondered if I belonged. But I kept going. Quietly.

milestones.title: Year 2 · The Breakthrough
milestones.body: Everything changed in Year 2. Better grades. I started speaking. My confidence grew. I stopped looking down on myself. I started collaborating. I started leading.

milestones.title: Building for Others
milestones.body: I build real systems. I lead a real community. I care about the people around RCA, not just the code inside it. I do this whether people are watching or not.

milestones.title: The Dream
milestones.body: MIT or Stanford. I've said it out loud to people who laughed. I still mean it. Faith is what got me here. Faith is what will take me further.

engineering.label: 04 · how I think about systems
engineering.title: Not just what I built.

cards.title: Academix: Why Microservices?
cards.body: I chose microservices for Academix not because tutorials said to, but because the domain genuinely had independent bounded contexts — students, records, and administration each change at different rates. What I didn't expect was how quickly distributed systems would introduce real complexity: authentication across services, shared data boundaries, and debugging across processes became engineering problems, not tutorial problems. That's when I understood the difference between reading about distributed systems and actually building one.

cards.title: MediSafe: AI in a High-Stakes Domain
cards.body: Building MediSafe forced me to think differently. When software is wrong in most domains, you lose data. When medication software is wrong, someone could be harmed. That responsibility shaped every decision: how I handled user input, how I validated drug interactions, how I designed the NLP prompts to stay within safe boundaries. Building for healthcare taught me that engineering is ethics.

engineering.terminalPrompt: $ what I learned building real systems
terminalLines: > monoliths are underrated until you need to scale
> distributed debugging is 10x harder than distributed design
> the best architecture is the one your team can reason about
> premature optimization is real, I've done it, it cost me time
> documentation is not optional, it is the system

growth.label: 03 · growth
growth.title: How I got here.
growth.subtitle: Not a contribution chart. A journal. Every node is a real moment — something built, something broken, something understood.

projects.label: 05 · things I built
projects.title: Real problems.

primary.name: Uptime
primary.tagline: Website monitoring that actually works. Multi-region. Multi-tenant. Fully monetized.
primary.desc:
I am building a monitoring platform from scratch.

The idea is simple: your website goes down at 3am, your team knows within seconds — not when a customer complains.

The hard part is not the monitoring itself. The hard part is not raising false alarms. If one region sees a failure but three others don't, that is not an outage. That is a network hiccup. Multi-region consensus is what separates real monitoring from noise.

Every company on the platform gets a public status page — the kind Stripe and GitHub have. Except I built mine from scratch, at Rwanda Coding Academy, in Year 2.

It has subscription billing. It is multi-tenant. It is the most serious thing I have ever built. I do not know if it will succeed. But I know it is real.
primary.arch:
→ monitoring workers   poll endpoints every 30–60s
→ multi-region nodes   consensus required for outage
→ alert dispatcher     Slack · Email · PagerDuty
→ status page engine   public · live · 90-day history
→ billing layer        subscription plans · multi-tenant
→ tenant isolation     each company fully separated
primary.closingUptime: This is not finished. But it is the most real thing I have ever built. I am a Year 2 student at Rwanda Coding Academy. I started this because I needed it to exist. That is usually a good enough reason.

primary.name: MediSafe
primary.tagline: Helping patients manage medications safely with AI.
primary.desc: Built for people living with chronic illness who struggle to track prescriptions and side effects every day. Healthcare software should feel like care, not complexity.
primary.arch: AI-powered · medication validation · natural language input
primary.evolution: MediSafe v1 → current: how it started, how it's going

primary.name: Academix
primary.tagline: A full microservices school management system.
primary.desc: Designed to bring efficiency to academic institutions. Students, records, workflows — all connected. Built to actually work at scale, not just in demos.

primary.name: furnit
primary.tagline: A furniture web experience built with care.
primary.desc: A frontend project where I focused purely on UI craft — clean design, smooth interactions, product feel.

secondary.name: Automailio
secondary.desc: An email automation platform — campaigns, templates, scheduling, delivery logs. Built with Java Spring Boot on the backend and React on the frontend. Containerized with Docker. This one taught me that building for reliability is a completely different challenge than building for functionality.

secondary.name: Enrollia
secondary.desc: An enrollment management dashboard built with Refine and Supabase. Handles data, access control, and admin workflows. The SQL schema is the part I'm most proud of — getting the data model right before writing a single line of React.

secondary.name: RFID System
secondary.desc: An RFID-based web system — hardware meets software. I built the web layer that connects to physical RFID readers. This came from a real problem we worked on at school. It's not perfect, but it's real, and it works. I also wrote C++ for Bluetooth communication and servo control — because I was curious, and curiosity doesn't wait for a syllabus.

secondary.name: MARKET-TRUTH
secondary.desc: A market information tool built in TypeScript. Someone forked it. That was the first time I realized something I built was useful to a stranger. It was a small moment, but it meant something.

secondary.name: Lumen
secondary.desc: Lumen — a full-stack TypeScript product with a separate frontend and backend. This is the most starred project on this account. I don't know exactly why people liked it, but I know I built it carefully. Both repos. Both mine.

secondary.name: Igihango Digital
secondary.desc: Igihango — a digital contract/agreement platform. 'Igihango' is Kinyarwanda for covenant or agreement. I built this for a Rwandan context because I believe software should solve problems where people actually are. JavaScript frontend, Python backend. Two repos, one idea.

secondary.name: Adheronix Demo
secondary.desc: Adheronix — a product demo built in TypeScript and deployed live. I built this to present something real. It is one of the larger things I've shipped. Not everything I build is for myself.

secondary.name: AI Resume Analyzer
secondary.desc: An AI tool that reads and analyzes resumes. I built this recently. It's not finished. But I believe in shipping things before they're perfect and learning from what breaks.

projects.closingNote:
These are some of what I've built.
There are 41 repositories across two GitHub accounts.
Some are unfinished. Some are experiments.
Some are things I'm not ready to talk about yet.
But every single one taught me something.
That's why I keep building.

mytime.label: 06 · my time
mytime.title: MY TIME
mytime.subtitle: Not dreams. Scheduled realities. Every second is turning potential into capability.
mytime.closing: The countdown is not to success. It is to who I become.

missions.title: MIT / Stanford Admission
missions.reflection: Preparing substance, not performance. Building proof before opportunity arrives.

missions.title: Speaking to MIT / Stanford Recruiters
missions.reflection: Not waiting to become valuable. Becoming undeniable.

missions.title: Joining MIT / Stanford
missions.reflection: The boy who imagined the sky is preparing to enter it.

missions.title: First Airplane Flight
missions.reflection: Some people wait for the future. I am building mine second by second.

missions.title: First Engineering Hiring
missions.reflection: Every passing second increases the distance between who I was and who I am becoming.

missions.title: Meeting a Sponsor
missions.reflection: I do not know if it will succeed. But I know it is real.

missions.title: First $1M Generated
missions.reflection: The countdown is not to success. It is to who I become.

community.label: 08 · beyond the code
community.title: A developer who only writes code
community.description: I believe RCA has a responsibility to the community around it, not just the students inside it. I organize outreach. I connect. I fight for people near us. I don't do this for recognition. I do it because it matters.
community.quoteLine2: > "Ibyo ntekereje mbikora abantu bandeba cyangwa batandeba."
community.quoteLine3: (What I do, I do whether people are watching or not.)
community.tag: Not for the CV. For real.

whyIBuild.label: 09 · the reason
whyIBuild.title: Why I Build
whyIBuild.line1: Because I know what it feels like to grow up without access.
whyIBuild.line2: Because I know what it feels like to sit in a room and doubt whether you belong there.
whyIBuild.line3: Because I watched technology completely redirect my life — from a quiet kid in Gatsibo who had never touched a PC, to someone building AI-powered systems and leading a community.
whyIBuild.line5: Now I want to build things that help it happen to someone else.

cards.label: lesson 01
cards.title: The first time tutorials weren't enough.
cards.body: I thought I understood Node.js because I could follow along. Then I tried to build something real and nothing worked the way the tutorial promised. That was the day I understood: knowing how to copy is not the same as knowing how to build.

cards.label: lesson 02
cards.title: The project I had to restart from scratch.
cards.body: I built a backend that worked on my machine and nowhere else. Wrong assumptions, wrong architecture, wrong approach. Restarting is painful. But it teaches you faster than anything else. I am not afraid of starting over anymore.

cards.label: lesson 03
cards.title: The moment I understood confidence is built, not given.
cards.body: I did not always believe I belonged in rooms like this. I was quiet because I was afraid. Not because I had nothing to say. Year 2 taught me: confidence is not a personality trait. It is a practice. I practice it every day.

obsession.label: 11 · what I'm chasing right now
thoughtsItems: > why do most systems fail at scale?
> can technology reduce inequality in Rwanda?
> how far can discipline take a person?
> what kind of engineer do I want to become?
> does the world need more builders or more thinkers?

becoming.label: 12 · trajectory
becoming.title: The engineer
paragraphs: I am not trying to become a job description.
I want to build systems that matter in healthcare and education — the two domains where software can most directly change lives.
I want to understand infrastructure deeply enough to build things that scale beyond my own imagination.
I want to eventually do research — at MIT or Stanford — on how distributed systems and AI can work together in low-resource, high-need environments like the ones I grew up in.
becoming.closing: I am a community-driven technologist. That is the engineer I am becoming.

cards.title: Faith
cards.body: Jesus Christ is the foundation of everything I've built and everything I am. Not performance. Not branding. Reality.

cards.title: Honesty
cards.body: This portfolio only contains things I have actually done. I would rather show you less and mean it, than show you more and fake it.

cards.title: Hard work
cards.body: I came from nothing on paper: no PC, no English, no connections. What I had was effort and prayer. It was enough to get me here. It will be enough to take me further.

cards.title: People
cards.body: Code is a tool. The goal is always people. I build software the way I lead community: not for the recognition, but for the impact.

contact.label: 14 · let's connect
contact.title: If you're building something meaningful, difficult, or human-centered — I would genuinely love to hear about it.
contact.alertMessage: Something went wrong. Please email me directly at mugishaivanbright250@gmail.com
contact.sponsorText: I am also looking for a sponsor who believes in where I'm going. If you've read this far and feel something — reach out. I will not waste your investment.

footer.builtWith: Built with faith, code, and too many prayers to count.
footer.location: Rwanda Coding Academy · Gatsibo · Eastern Province · Rwanda 🇷🇼
